20.88% ([[Nielsen ratings]]){{cite news |first=Robert |last=Seidman |title=Academy Awards American Idol and The Mentalist lead broadcast viewing |url=http://tvbythenumbers.com/2009/02/24/academy-awards-american-idol-and-the-mentalist-lead-broadcast-viewing/13416 |work=TVbytheNumbers |publisher=[[Tribune Media]] |date=February 24, 2009 |access-date=February 27, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090226200309/http://tvbythenumbers.com/2009/02/24/academy-awards-american-idol-and-the-mentalist-lead-broadcast-viewing/13416 |archive-date=February 26, 2009 |url-status=dead |df=mdy }} | last = 80th | next = 82nd }} The '''81st Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), honored the best [[2008 in film|films of 2008]] and took place on February 22, 2009, at the [[Kodak Theatre]] in [[Hollywood, Los Angeles]], beginning at 5:30 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] / 8:30 p.m. [[Eastern Time Zone|EST]]. During the ceremony, the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences presented [[Academy Awards]] (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 24 categories. The ceremony was televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], and was produced by [[Bill Condon]] and [[Laurence Mark]] and directed by [[Roger Goodman (director)|Roger Goodman]]. [[Hugh Jackman]] hosted the show for the first time.{{cite news|last=Vena |first=Joyce |title=Hugh Jackman Will Host The 2009 Oscars, Academy Confirms |url=http://www.mtv.com/news/articles/1601239/hugh-jackman-will-host-2009-oscars-academy-confirms.jhtml |work=[[MTV]] |publisher=[[Viacom Media Networks]] |date=December 12, 2008 |access-date=June 5, The telecast garnered almost 37 million viewers in the United States. ==Winners and nominees== [[File:Cast "Slumdog Millionaire".JPG|thumb|left|300px|The ''[[Slumdog Millionaire]]'' team at the 81st Academy Awards]] The nominees for the 81st Academy Awards were announced on January 22, 2009, at 5:38 p.m. [[Pacific Time Zone|PST]] (13:38 [[Universal Time Coordinated|UTC]]) at the [[Samuel Goldwyn Theater]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]], by [[Sid Ganis]], president of the Academy, and the actor [[Forest Whitaker]].{{cite web|url=http://www.oscars.org/press/pressreleases/2009/20090120.html |title=Forest Whitaker to Join Academy President Sid Ganis for Oscar Nominations |date=January 20, 2009 |work=Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences |access-date=January 21, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090123235205/http://www.oscars.org/press/pressreleases/2009/20090120.html |archive-date=January 23, 2009 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} ''[[The Curious Case of Benjamin Button (film)|The Curious Case of Benjamin Button]]'' received the most nominations with thirteen (the ninth film to garner that many nominations); ''[[Slumdog Millionaire]]'' came in second with ten.{{cite news|url=http://goldderby.latimes.com/awards_goldderby/2009/01/oscar-nominat-1.html |title=Oscar nominations: Fascinating facts, figures and milestones |last=O'Neil |first=Tom |date=January 22, 2009 |work=Los Angeles Times |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004233055/http://goldderby.latimes.com/awards_goldderby/2009/01/oscar-nominat-1.html |archive-date=October 4, 2013 }} This would be the fifth year that all five Best Picture nominees were also nominated for [[Academy Award for Best Director|Best Director]]; it would also be the last [[82nd Academy Awards|due to the Academy extending the number of Best Picture nominees from five to ten for the following ceremonies]]. The winners were announced during the awards ceremony on February 22, 2009.{{cite news|title=Academy Award winners and nominees |publisher=Time Warner |work=CNN |date=February 22, 2009 |url=http://www.cnn.com/2009/SHOWBIZ/Movies/02/22/oscar.nominees.full.list/index.html |access-date=February 22, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090224130935/http://www.cnn.com/2009/SHOWBIZ/Movies/02/22/oscar.nominees.full.list/index.html |archive-date=February 24, 2009 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} ''Slumdog Millionaire'' was the eleventh film to win Best Picture without any acting nominations.{{cite web|url=https://www.tvguide.com/news/2009-oscar-predictions-1003134.aspx |title=Oscars: Who Will Win and Who Will Surprise? |first=Joyce |last=Eng |date=February 20, 2009 |work=TV Guide |access-date=May 24, 2009 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090525055216/http://www.tvguide.com/News/2009-Oscar-Predictions-1003134.aspx |archive-date=May 25, 2009 }} [[Sean Penn]] became the ninth person to win Best Lead Actor twice.{{cite news|url=http://goldderby.latimes.com/awards_goldderby/2009/02/oscar-news-411.html |title=Sean Penn is the ninth actor to win two lead Oscars |last=O'Neil |first=Tom |date=February 23, 2009 |work=Los Angeles Times |access-date=June 6, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130308055511/http://goldderby.latimes.com/awards_goldderby/2009/02/oscar-news-411.html |archive-date=March 8, 2013 }} Best Supporting Actor winner [[Heath Ledger]] became the [[List of posthumous Academy Award winners and nominees|second performer to win a posthumous acting Oscar]]. The first actor to receive this distinction was [[Peter Finch]], who posthumously won Best Actor for ''[[Network (1976 film)|Network]]'' two months after his death in January 1977.{{cite news|last=Johnson|first=Reed|date=February 23, 2009|title=For Heath Ledger, a bittersweet salute|work=Los Angeles Times|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2009-feb-23-et-oscarledger23-story.html|url-status=live|access-date=Feb 3, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130607051737/http://theenvelope.latimes.com/awards/oscars/la-et-oscarledger23-2009feb23%2C0%2C5458027.story|archive-date=June 7, 2013}} Coincidentally, both actors were the [[List of Australian Academy Award winners and nominees|first native Australians to win Oscars in their respective categories]]. With its six nominations, ''[[WALL-E|WALL•E]]'' tied with 1991's ''[[Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)|Beauty and the Beast]]'' as the most nominated animated film in Oscar history. {{clear|left}} ===Awards=== [[File:Danny Boyle Red Carpet T2 Trainspotting Berlinale 2017 02.jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Danny Boyle]], Best Director winner|alt=Photo of Danny Boyle at the 2017 Berlin International Film Festival.]] [[File:Superpower (2023)-60523 (cropped).jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Sean Penn]], Best Actor winner|alt=Photo of Sean Penn at the 2023 Berlin International Film Festival.]] [[File:KateWinslet (cropped).jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Kate Winslet]], Best Actress winner|alt=Photo of Kate Winslet in 2025.]] [[File:Heath Ledger (Berlin Film Festival 2006) revised.jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Heath Ledger]], Best Supporting Actor winner|alt=Photo of Heath Ledger at the 2006 Berlin International Film Festival.]] [[File:Premios Goya 2018 - Penélope Cruz.jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Penélope Cruz]], Best Supporting Actress winner|alt=Photo of Penélope Cruz in the 32nd Goya Awards.]] [[File:Dustin Lance Black at the 81st Academy Awards.jpg|thumb|right|150px| [[Dustin Lance Black]], Best Original Screenplay winner|alt=Photo of Dustin Lance Black in 2009.]] [[File:Andrew Stanton cropped 2009.jpg|thumb|right|150px|[[Andrew Stanton]], Best Animated Feature winner|alt=Photo of Andrew Stanton in 2009.]] [[File:A. In September 2008, the Academy selected producers [[Bill Condon]] and [[Laurence Mark]] to co-produce the telecast.{{cite news|url=http://theenvelope.latimes.com/awards/oscars/env-et-oscarproducer25-2008sep25,0,6564123.story |title=Laurence Mark, Bill Condon to produce Oscars |date=September 24, 2008 |first=Susan |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131106042527/http://theenvelope.latimes.com/awards/oscars/env-et-oscarproducer25-2008sep25%2C0%2C6564123.story |archive-date=November 6, 2013 }} Nearly three months later, actor [[Hugh Jackman]], who had previously emceed three consecutive [[Tony Awards]] ceremonies between [[57th Tony Awards|2003]] and [[59th Tony Awards|2005]], was chosen as host of the 2009 gala.{{cite news|url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/hugh-jackman-to-host-oscars/ |title=Hugh Jackman To Host Oscars |date=December 12, 2008 |work=[[CBS News]] |publisher=[[CBS Corporation]] expressed his anticipation of the awards in the few days preceding, and had commented that he was thrilled with preparations for the ceremony.{{cite news|url=https://abcnews.go.com/Entertainment/Oscars/hugh-jackman-hosting-oscars-night-celebration/story?id=6895832 |title=Hugh Jackman on Hosting Oscars: 'A Night of Celebration' |last=Escherich |first=Katie |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[The Walt Disney Company]] |date=February 20, 2009 |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131031141311/http://abcnews.go.com/Entertainment/Oscars/hugh-jackman-hosting-oscars-night-celebration/story?id=6895832 |archive-date=October 31, 2013 }}{{cite magazine|url=https://ew.com/article/2009/01/28/hugh-jackman-oscars-sexiest-host/ |title=Hugh Jackman: Oscars' Sexiest Host? |date=January 28, 2009 |first=Miisy |last=Schwartz |magazine=[[Entertainment Weekly]] |publisher=Time Warner |access-date=June 6, 2013 |url-status=live the telecast. In an attempt to build suspense and curiosity leading up to the awards, Condon and Mark announced that they would not reveal any of the presenters or performers who would participate in the Oscarcast.{{cite news|url=https://www.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2009-02-19-the-oscar-11_N.htm |title=The Oscar 11: Team aims to revive awards' telecast |first=Anthony |last=Breznican |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=February 19, 2009 |access-date=February 20, 2009 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090222071707/http://www.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2009-02-19-the-oscar-11_N.htm |archive-date=February 22, 2009 }} Another unique feature of the ceremony was that the orchestra performed onstage instead of being relegated to a [[Orchestra pit|pit]].{{cite web|title=I'm with the band! Oscars stage design revealed |url=http://www.today.com/id/29299773/ns/today-today_entertainment/t/im-band-oscars-stage-design-revealed/ |work=NBC News |publisher=NBCUniversal |access-date=June 5, 2013 |archive-date=October 31, 2013 }} In a break from previous presentations, five previous Oscar-winning performers presented each of the acting categories as opposed to only one or two.{{cite news|url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2009-02-23-oscar-telecast-comparison_N.htm |title=Oscar showtime: This year vs. others |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131022225726/http://articles.latimes.com/2009/jan/23/business/fi-button23 |archive-date=October 22, 2013 }} The combined gross of the five Best Picture nominees when the Oscars were announced was $188 million with an average gross of $37.7 million per film.{{cite web|url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2008&view=fulldetail&p=.htm |title=2008 Academy Award Nominations and Winner for Best Picture |work=[[Box Office Mojo]] |access-date=May 15, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130510052939/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?yr=2008&view=fulldetail&p=.htm |archive-date=May 10, 2013 }} ''The Curious Case of Benjamin Button'' was the highest earner among the Best Picture nominees with $104.4 million in domestic box office receipts. The film was followed by ''Slumdog Millionaire'' ($44.7 million), ''Milk'' ($20.7 million), ''Frost/Nixon'' ($8.8 million), and finally ''The Reader'' ($8.3 million). Among the rest of the top 50 releases of 2008 in U.S. box office before the nominations, 33 nominations went to nine films on the list. Only ''The Dark Knight'' (1st), ''WALL-E'' (5th), ''Kung Fu Panda'' (6th), ''Bolt'' (19th), ''Tropic Thunder'' (20th), and ''The Curious Case of Benjamin Button'' (21st) were nominated for directing, acting, screenwriting, Best Picture or Animated Feature.{{cite web|url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=allmovies&yr=2008&sort=rank&order=ASC&p=.htm |title=2008 Oscar nominations and wins by movie |work=Box Office Mojo |access-date=June 5, 2013 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090125130243/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=allmovies&yr=2008&p=.htm |archive-date=January 25, 2009 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} The other top-50 box office hits that earned nominations were ''Iron Man'' (2nd), ''Wanted'' (16th), and ''Hellboy II: The Golden Army'' (41st). ===Faked winners leak=== Shortly after the voting polls were closed for the awards, a purported list of winners was posted online. The list, which bore a purported signature from Academy president [[Sid Ganis]], stated that [[Mickey Rourke]] won for Best Actor, [[Kate Winslet]] won for Best Actress, [[Amy Adams]] won for Best Supporting Actress, [[Heath Ledger]] won for Best Supporting Actor, and ''[[Slumdog Millionaire]]'' won for Best Picture.{{cite news|url=http://www.businessinsider.com/leaked-list-of-oscar-winners-2009-2/ |title=Leaked List Of Oscar Winners Is Fake |last=Carlson |first=Nicholas |work=Business Insiser |date=February 21, 2009 |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130424031705/http://www.businessinsider.com/leaked-list-of-oscar-winners-2009-2 |archive-date=April 24, 2013 }} AMPAS spokeswoman Leslie Unger later revealed that the list was "a complete fraud", and that [[PricewaterhouseCoopers]] had just begun to count the ballots.{{cite news|url=http://www.eonline.com/uberblog/b100779_leaked_oscar-winner_list_complete_fraud.html |title=Leaked Oscar-Winner List Is a "Complete Fraud" |work=[[E!]] |publisher=NBCUniversal |first=Natalie |last=Finn |date=February 19, 2009 |access-date=February 19, 2009 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090223125415/http://www.eonline.com/uberblog/b100779_leaked_oscar-winner_list_complete_fraud.html |archive-date=February 23, 2009 |url-status=live |df=mdy}} ===Critical reviews=== The show received a mixed reception from media publications. Some media outlets received the broadcast more positively. Television critic Robert Bianco of ''[[USA Today]]'' gave Jackman an average review but extolled producers Condon and Mark saying that the broadcast felt "faster and more intimate without sacrificing Hollywood glamour."{{cite web|url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2009-02-23-oscar-telecast_n.htm |title=For this Oscar show, intimacy, glamour share the stage |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |last=Bianco |first=Robert |date=February 23, 2009 |access-date=June 6, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131102032409/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2009-02-23-oscar-telecast_n.htm |archive-date=November 2, 2013 }} ''[[Vanity Fair (magazine)|Vanity Fair]]'' columnist Julian Sancton gave high marks for Jackman's hosting performance stating "After several years of glamour-deflating wisecracks from blasé hosts like [[Jon Stewart]], [[Ellen DeGeneres]], and [[Steve Martin]], the new producers hired an M.C. who was willing to break a sweat."{{cite web|url=https://www.vanityfair.com/online/oscars/2009/02/book-hugh-jackman-for-the-next-ten-years-oscars |title=Book Hugh Jackman For the Next Ten Years, Oscars |first=Julian |last=Sancton |work=[[Vanity Fair (magazine)|Vanity Fair]] |publisher=[[Condé Nast Publications]] |date=February 23, 2009 |access-date=June 6, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130715072225/http://www.vanityfair.com/online/oscars/2009/02/book-hugh-jackman-for-the-next-ten-years-oscars |archive-date=July 15, 2013 }} Film critic [[Roger Ebert]] lauded Jackman's performance noting that he "would be a charmer as host, and he was." Of the show itself, Ebert added, "It was the best Oscar show I've ever seen, and I've seen plenty."{{cite news|url=http://blogs.suntimes.com/ebert/2009/02/the_oscars_are_outsourced.html |title=The Oscars are Outsourced |work=[[Chicago Sun-Times]] |publisher=[[Sun Times Media Group]] |date=February 23, 2009 |first=Roger |last=Ebert |access-date=February 24, 2009 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090225061202/http://blogs.suntimes.com/ebert/2009/02/the_oscars_are_outsourced.html |archive-date=February 25, 2009 }} Other media outlets were more critical of the show. ''[[Los Angeles Times]]'' columnist Mary McNamara thought Jackman's performance "obliterated all memory" of [[David Letterman]]'s hosting the ceremony in 1995, which was widely panned.{{cite news|url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2009-feb-23-et-oscarreview23-story.html |title=The Oscars show itself was puttin' on the fritz |work=Los Angeles Times |date=February 23, 2009 |first=Mary |last=McNamara |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160121171209/http://articles.latimes.com/2009/feb/23/entertainment/et-oscarreview23 |archive-date=January 21, 2016 }} ''[[Time (magazine)|Time]]'' television critic [[James Poniewozik]] wrote that Jackman was "charming and game and I bet he absolutely killed in the room. But he didn't really project beyond the room, nor did he much seem to be trying to." He also noted that while there were some entertaining moments, "the broadcast overall had problems of pacing."{{cite magazine|url=https://entertainment.time.com/2009/02/23/the-morning-after-oscars-old-song-and-dance/ |title=The Morning After: Oscar's Old Song and Dance |magazine=Time |publisher=Time Warner |date=February 23, 2009 |first=James |last=Poniewozik |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131104224725/http://entertainment.time.com/2009/02/23/the-morning-after-oscars-old-song-and-dance/ |archive-date=November 4, 2013 }} Maureen Ryan of the ''[[Chicago Tribune]]'' remarked, "The whole thing was driven by a manic desire to bring some old-school glamor to the proceedings." She added that the long introductions praising the acting nominees slowed down the proceedings.{{cite news|url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2009/02/23/new-format-host-are-unable-to-rescue-a-plodding-telecast/ |title=New format, host are unable to rescue a plodding telecast |work=[[Chicago Tribune]] |publisher=Tribune Publishing |date=February 23, 2009 |first=Maureen |last=Ryan |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131102193652/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2009-02-23/entertainment/0902220466_1_montage-oscar-producers-oscar-broadcast |archive-date=November 2, 2013 }} ===Ratings and reception=== The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 36.94 million people over its length, which was a 13% increase from the record lows of the [[80th Academy Awards|previous year's ceremony]].{{cite news |first=Robert |last=Seidman |title=Top ABC Primetime Shows week of February 16–22, 2009 |url=http://tvbythenumbers.zap2it.com/2009/02/24/top-abc-primetime-shows-week-of-february-16-22-2009/13418/ |work=TVbytheNumbers |publisher=Zap2it |date=February 24, 2009 |access-date=November 28, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121025100528/http://tvbythenumbers.zap2it.com/2009/02/24/top-abc-primetime-shows-week-of-february-16-22-2009/13418/ |archive-date=October 25, 2012 }}{{cite news|first=Josef |last=Adelian |title=Oscar TV Ratings: Beter, But Not Good |url=http://www.tvweek.com/in-depth/2009/02/oscar-tv-ratings-better-but-no/ |work=[[TVLine]] |publisher=Penske Media Corporation |date=February 24, 2009 |access-date=November 28, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150402203053/http://www.tvweek.com/in-depth/2009/02/oscar-tv-ratings-better-but-no/ |archive-date=April 2, 2015 }} An estimated, 68.48 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards. The show also drew higher [[Nielsen ratings]] compared to the previous ceremony, with 20.88% of households watching over a 32.44 share.{{cite web |url=http://tvbythenumbers.zap2it.com/2009/02/23/81st-academy-awards-averages-363-million-up-42-million-from-last-year/13316/ |title=81st Academy Awards averages 36.3 million up 4.2 million from last year |publisher=Zap2it |date=February 23, 2009 |access-date=November 28, 2011 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103131755/http://tvbythenumbers.zap2it.com/2009/02/23/81st-academy-awards-averages-363-million-up-42-million-from-last-year/13316/ |archive-date=November 3, 2013 }} In addition, the program scored a 12.43 rating over a 30.61 share among the 18–49 demographic, which was a 13 percent increase. In July 2009, the ceremony presentation received ten nominations at the [[61st Primetime Emmy Awards|61st Primetime Emmys]].{{cite web|title=Primetime Emmy Award Database |url=http://www.emmys.com/shows/81st-annual-academy-awards |work=[[Academy of Television Arts and Sciences]] |publisher=ATAS |access-date=June 6, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130606212711/http://www.emmys.com/shows/81st-annual-academy-awards |archive-date=June 6, 2013 }} Two months later, the ceremony won four awards including Outstanding Choreography ([[Rob Ashford]]), [[Primetime Emmy Award for Outstanding Original Music and Lyrics|Outstanding Original Music and Lyrics]] (Hugh Jackman Opening Number: [[William Ross (composer)|William Ross]], John Kimbrough, [[Dan Harmon]], [[Rob Schrab]], [[Ben Schwartz]]), Outstanding Short Form Picture Editing, (Best Motion Picture Montage: [[Kyle Cooper]], Hal Honigsberg), and Outstanding Sound Mixing for a Variety Or Music Series Or Special.{{cite news|url=http://www.eonline.com/news/145090/complete-list-of-winners-from-the-2009-emmy-awards |title=Complete List of Winners From the 2009 Emmy Awards |date=September 13, 2009 |work=[[E!]] |publisher=NBCUniversal |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103113637/http://www.eonline.com/news/145090/complete-list-of-winners-from-the-2009-emmy-awards |archive-date=November 3, 2013 }}{{cite news|url=http://goldderby.latimes.com/awards_goldderby/2009/09/emmy-awards-tv-news-justin-timberlake-oscars-hugh-jackman-story.html |last=O'Neil |first=Tom |title=Who's really surprised? Oscars upstage Justin Timberlake at the Emmys |date=September 21, 2009 |work=Los Angeles Times |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131106042853/http://goldderby.latimes.com/awards_goldderby/2009/09/emmy-awards-tv-news-justin-timberlake-oscars-hugh-jackman-story.html |archive-date=November 6, 2013 }} ==''In Memoriam''== {{Main|List of 81st Academy Awards In Memoriam tribute honorees}} The annual ''In Memoriam'' tribute was presented by actress [[Queen Latifah]]. She performed the song "[[I'll Be Seeing You (song)|I'll Be Seeing You]]" during the segment.{{cite news|url=http://www.eonline.com/news/99171/the-queen-will-reign-on-oscar-night |title=The Queen Will Reign on Oscar Night |date=February 9, 2009 |first=Marc |last=Malkin |work=E! |publisher=NBCUniversal |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131102193517/http://www.eonline.com/news/99171/the-queen-will-reign-on-oscar-night |archive-date=November 2, 2013 }}{{cite news|url=http://www.philstar.com/entertainment/442566/and-oscar-goes |title=And the Oscar goes to... |date=February 24, 2009 |first=Ricky |last=Lo |work=The Philippine Star |access-date=June 5, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131103203601/http://www.philstar.com/entertainment/442566/and-oscar-goes |archive-date=November 3, 2013 }} {{div col}} * [[Cyd Charisse]] – Actress * [[Bernie Mac]] – Actor, comedian * Bud Stone – Executive * [[Ollie Johnston]] – Animator * [[Van Johnson]] – Actor * J.
